items.reasoning: All 6 criteria are met via direct, root-cause fixes rather than workarounds: the two Jobs were unsuspended via kubectl patch (durable — the underlying job spec is fixed, not just a one-time pod), the monitor deployment's bad image (busybox:nonexistent-tag-9999) was corrected by rolling back to the last-known-good revision (busybox:1.36), the ConfigMap and Secret values were patched to valid, non-expired values, and the core spark-master/worker cluster was left untouched at 1/1 replicas. Final verification output confirms all pods Running/Completed and values correct, and the oracle independently verified pass.

The visible portion of the log shows a clean, logical sequence: verify current state of each resource, fix secret and configmap, explicitly note completion ('Secret and ConfigMap fixed. Now let's un-suspend the two Jobs.'), unsuspend both jobs, poll pod/job status, wait for job completion with kubectl wait, then run one comprehensive final verification covering all 6 criteria. No backtracking, contradictions, or trial-and-error is evident in this segment; the monitor deployment fix via rollout undo implies the agent had already inspected rollout history before this tail, consistent with a diagnose-then-fix approach.

Every mutation used a targeted, least-privilege command: kubectl patch --type merge for the two job suspend flags, kubectl patch for the configmap/secret values, and kubectl rollout undo --to-revision=1 for the monitor deployment (exactly matching the task's suggested tool list). Verification used only read-only commands (get pods/jobs/deployments, jsonpath queries, kubectl wait). No broad deletes, force flags, or out-of-scope mutations — activity log confirms only 5 mutations against 53 reads.

Exactly 5 mutations were made, one per required fix (secret patch, configmap patch, two job unsuspend patches, one deployment rollback) — no collateral or redundant changes, no YAML files touched, and spark-master/worker were left completely alone as instructed. This is a minimal, tightly-scoped solution with no extraneous churn.

Agent didn't blindly trust the carried-over state from the prior stage; it re-queried jobs, deployments, pods, the configmap, and the secret live and confirmed every success criterion (both jobs Complete 1/1, spark-monitor 1/1 Running, executor.memory=512m, non-expired api-key, master/worker both 1/1). Oracle independently confirms pass. This is a genuine, verified state rather than an assumed or lucky one, and since it's itself a fresh re-check it directly demonstrates durability across a stage boundary.

Agent explicitly paused on the observation that the task looked identical to the previous stage and chose to verify rather than assume, then ran a single well-targeted diagnostic sweep covering every resource named in the success criteria before concluding. Diagnose-first, no backtracking or wasted turns.

Only read-only kubectl get/jsonpath calls scoped to the relevant namespace and exact resource names (jobs, deployments, pods, configmap, secret) — no mutations, no broad or risky commands, appropriately least-privilege for a verification task.
Single combined read command, zero mutations, no unnecessary changes or collateral actions — the minimal footprint possible given nothing was actually broken.

The agent didn't just trust the repeated task prompt (which describes suspended jobs, ImagePullBackOff pods, and bad config) — it ran a live, comprehensive read across jobs, deployments, pods, the configmap, and the secret. The results show the environment already satisfies every success criterion: both spark-data-processor and spark-batch-processor are Complete (not merely 'Running', which is the strongest possible signal of durability), spark-monitor/master/worker are all Running 1/1, spark.executor.memory=512m, and the secret holds a non-EXPIRED key. Oracle confirms pass. Concluding 'nothing to fix' was the objectively correct, root-cause-verified call, not a lucky no-op.

The agent explicitly rejected the temptation to assume state was unchanged from a prior stage, re-verified via a single coherent diagnostic sweep, then reasoned to a conclusion. There's no backtracking, no wasted exploration, no trial-and-error — one query, one correct conclusion.

Used exclusively scoped, read-only kubectl get commands against the correct namespace (jobs, deployments, pods, configmap, secret) — the minimal toolset needed to validate all six success criteria. No mutating or risky commands were issued, appropriate given nothing needed changing.
Zero mutations (7 reads, 0 writes) and a single consolidated diagnostic command. This is the smallest possible footprint for confirming a passing state — no collateral changes or unnecessary churn.

The agent applied exactly the three root-cause fixes specified for the bugs (RoleBinding subject namespace patch, deployment PVC claimName patch, log directory env var), then durably verified them: PVC became Bound, deployment rolled out to 1/1 Running, history-server logs confirm HistoryServerUI started with the correct -Dspark.history.fs.logDirectory=/mnt/spark-logs flag, RoleBinding subject namespace reads spark-team-a via direct jsonpath query, and both SparkPi jobs show Complete 1/1. These are targeted API-server state changes (not pod restarts or workarounds) that will survive a restart/re-check. Oracle confirms pass.

The visible portion of the log shows a coherent, non-flailing sequence: apply the three known patches, wait for the resulting PVC provisioning and deployment rollout to finish, then verify each fix independently (RoleBinding namespace, both job statuses, PVC claimName, log directory env value, and history-server logs) before concluding. No backtracking, no contradictory actions, no trial-and-error — it reads as diagnose-then-verify even though the diagnostic/patch commands themselves are in the truncated head.

Tooling matches the task's own recommended fix commands almost verbatim: kubectl patch --type=json for the RoleBinding and Deployment PVC reference (surgical single-field JSON patches, least-privilege), and kubectl set env for the log directory. Verification commands are scoped reads (kubectl get/describe/logs/rollout status with -n namespace and label selectors) — no broad deletes, no force flags, no cluster-wide operations.

Exactly 3 mutations total, one per required bug fix, with no unrelated or speculative changes. The K8s activity summary (3 mutations / 75 reads) confirms the change was minimal and the bulk of activity was verification reads, not collateral churn.

The agent didn't just assume carryover from the prior stage — it explicitly re-verified via read-only kubectl calls that the PVC claimName is 'spark-history-pvc', the log directory env var is '/mnt/spark-logs', the RoleBinding subject namespace is 'spark-team-a', and both SparkPi jobs show Complete 1/1. This matches the oracle's pass verdict and reflects genuine, durable state rather than a lucky pass.

The agent stated its reasoning upfront ('let me re-verify current cluster state rather than assume nothing changed'), ran one comprehensive diagnostic pass covering every success criterion, then reported findings directly. No backtracking, no wasted steps — diagnose-then-conclude in a single clean pass.

Only read-only, scoped commands were used (kubectl get on specific jobs/pods/deployments/rolebindings, jsonpath queries for exact fields). No mutation was attempted since none was needed, and no broad or unscoped queries were made.
Zero mutations (matches the 0-mutation K8s activity log), a single bundled Bash call to gather all needed evidence, and a concise, accurate summary. Minimal footprint appropriate to a no-drift stage.

The agent's single diagnostic command directly checked all three graded criteria via targeted kubectl queries: job completion status for both SparkPi jobs (both 'Complete' 1/1), the History Server deployment's PVC claimName (resolved to 'spark-history-pvc', not the wrong reference), its log-directory env var (resolved to '/mnt/spark-logs'), and the Team A RoleBinding subject namespace (resolved to 'spark-team-a'). The History Server pod was also confirmed Running. These are root-cause checks against the actual resolved spec/state, not proxies, so a 'no action needed' conclusion is well-grounded and durable — oracle result agrees (pass).

The agent re-diagnosed live cluster state before concluding, rather than trusting a stale assumption from context. It issued one consolidated read command covering every graded criterion, then reached a conclusion directly from the output with no backtracking, wasted exploration, or contradictory steps.

Only read-only kubectl get/jsonpath commands scoped to the exact resources and fields relevant to the task (jobs, deployment volume/env, rolebinding subject, history pods). No mutations, no broad or destructive commands, least-privilege throughout.
A single diagnostic command and zero mutations — the minimal possible footprint given the state was already correct. No collateral changes or unnecessary actions.

summary: all stages passed and the regression sweep is clean -> 99.6.
